coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of silver ions in different pH conditions using Pongamia pinnata leaf extract derived carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sunitha Devi, Balathoti
Laxmi, Bugude
Linga Raju, Ch.
Rosaiah, Gorrepati
Seshadri Reddy, Ankireddy
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (PPCDs) with OH and COOH functional groups were synthesized using Pongamia pinnata leaf extract as a carbon source by oxidation and a hydrothermal process. The colorimetric sensing results reveal the intensity of PPCDs enhancement upon the addition of the different concentrations of the silver ions, with LODs of 7.8, 0.758, and 0.340 μM in 5.1, 7.2, and 10 pH conditions, respectively. On the other hand, point-of-care kit (POC) data exhibits an intensive band that appeared after the addition of the silver ions at three pH conditions. The formation of the OH and COOH functionalities on the surface of the PPCDs might be responsible for the highly selective and sensitive detection of the Ag+ ions through metal–ligand charge transfer complex formation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silver ions sensing
POC detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Reaction scheme for the synthesis of PPCDs, (B) morphological observations, (C) optical properties, (D) functional group analysis, and (E) phase purity and crystallinity before and after treatment with silver ions.

Sensitivity analysis of Ag+ ions at (A) pH-5.1, (B) pH-7.2, and (C) pH-10, (D) linearity response curves at three different pH conditions, and (E) selectivity analysis with various metal ions of (E) line curve and (F) bar chart.

(A) POC kit, (B) kinetic parameters, (C) band gap calculations, functional group analysis at 3000–3800 cm−1 (D), 1700–1800 cm−1 (E), 1200–1500 cm−1 (F), 800–900 cm−1 (G) specific regions before and after treatment of Ag+ ions, and possible working mechanism for silver ions detection (H).

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of silver ions in different pH conditions using Pongamia pinnata leaf extract derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany and Microbiology, Acharya Nagarjuna University, Guntur 522510, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Botany and Microbiology, Acharya Nagarjuna University, Guntur 522510, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, Dr. Buddolla’s Institute of Life Sciences, Tirupati 517503, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. Buddolla’s Institute of Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, Dr. Buddolla’s Institute of Life Sciences, Tirupati 517503, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Acharya Nagarjuna University, Guntur 522510, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Acharya Nagarjuna University, Guntur 522510, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (PPCDs) with OH and COOH functional groups were synthesized using Pongamia pinnata leaf extract as a carbon source by oxidation and a hydrothermal process. The colorimetric sensing results reveal the intensity of PPCDs enhancement upon the addition of the different concentrations of the silver ions, with LODs of 7.8, 0.758, and 0.340 μM in 5.1, 7.2, and 10 pH conditions, respectively. On the other hand, point-of-care kit (POC) data exhibits an intensive band that appeared after the addition of the silver ions at three pH conditions. The formation of the OH and COOH functionalities on the surface of the PPCDs might be responsible for the highly selective and sensitive detection of the Ag+ ions through metal–ligand charge transfer complex formation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the oxidation–reduction characteristics of silver ions, which may result in a range of human disorders, their detection is an important task [1–6]. Even at a very nanogram level, silver ions and silver nanoparticles have an impact on the aquatic environment [7]. The COOH and OH functional groups typically play a crucial part in encapsulating the metal ions [8–10]. However, there are currently no studies on the colorimetric detection of silver ions using carbon dots under the three pH values of acidic, neutral, and basic. In contrast, utilising POC to detect silver ions is one of the most practical methods compared to other methods now in use. A leaf extract from Pongamia pinnata was used in this research to synthesize carbon dots with functional groups for OH and COOH. For the very accurate and sensitive detection of silver ions at pH levels of 5.1, 7.2, and 10, a colorimetric technique was developed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the calorimetric detection of low LOD silver ions under three different pH settings. A POC kit was also developed to detect silver ions at incredibly low quantities.

The healthy leaves of P. pinnata were collected in Tirumala Hills, India, and washed with DI water. The dried leaves were then turned into an aqueous extract using the Soxhlet method. Following oxidation with KMnO4 and H2O2, the reaction mixture was placed into an autoclave lined with Teflon for a hydrothermal reaction to produce COOH and OH groups. The Teflon-lined hydrothermal was then heated for 12 h at 160 °C. Later, PPCDs were isolated by ultracentrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. By adding HCl and NaOH, the PPCDs stock solutions were produced at pH values of 5.1, 7.2, and 10. Later, colorimetric sensing was developed by adding50 μL of a series of silver ion concentrations (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16and 18 μM) with 1 mL of PPCDs solution (0.5 mg/mL as a stock solution) and incubating for 3 min to allow for the reaction. Colorimetric changes were observed using an Eppendorf BioUV–vis spectrophotometer. Furthermore, a selectivity experiment was carried out at pH 7.4 in which 100 μLof 20 μMindividual metal ion solutions were introduced into a cuvette containing 1 mL of PPCDs solution, incubated for 5 min, and the results were recorded. As a POC kit platform, aluminum-coated silica plate was used. Here, a needle was used to apply PPCD solutions to the silica plate at various pH levels. The results were then analyzed after 10 min of incubation with 25 μMof the AgNO3 solution on the silica plate.

Using leaf extract with COOH and OH functions and a hydrothermal method to detect the silver ions at low concentrations, PPCDs were synthesized. Then, as shown in Fig. 1 A, PPCDs were used to fabricate a POC kit to detect silver ions as well as to sense silver ions at three distinct pH settings. Later, as-prepared PPCDs were compared to those that had silver ion treatment. The PPCDs' morphology was examined using TEM, and it was found that the nanoparticles were consistently generated and ranged in size from 3 to 5 nm (Fig. 1B). The lattice fringes of the nanoparticle with 0.319 nm inter-lattice spacing are visible in the inset image as a distinctive characteristic of the (002) lattice plane of a graphitic facet. As shown in Fig. 1C (curve i), the presence ofπ-π* and n-π*electronic transitions at 265 and 320 nm, respectively, has resulted in the appearance of two distinctive absorption bands in the PP leaf extract. A similar kind of peak was also identified in PPCDs (Fig. 1C, curve ii) with a slight red shift. However, after the addition of the Ag+ ions (50 μL of 10 μM/mL) (Fig. 1C, curve iii), both peaks disappeared and two new peaks were formed. This may be due to the presence of π-π* electronic transition and the ligand metal charge transfer complex (LMCT) at 255 and 410 nm, respectively. The inset figure shows the colorimetric changes in the samples, with the PP extract having a green hue and the PPCDs having pale yellow and dark brown hues, respectively, before and after the addition of Ag+ ions.

According to FTIR, PP leaf extract displayed a number of absorption bands at 3560, 2985, 2845, 1625, and 1080 cm−1 indicated the existence of OH, ArCH, CH, CC, and CO stretching frequencies, respectively (Fig. 1D, green curve). Similar peaks were obtained after the synthesis of PPCDs (Fig. 1D, brown curve), with a minor peak shift towards higher wavelengths. The development of the COOH group on the surface of the PPCDs was confirmed by the emergence of a new peak for carboxylic acid at 1735 cm−1. After the addition of the Ag+ ions, the PPCD peaks showed significant peak shiftings, indicating that the silver ions interacted with the COOH and OH functions (Fig. 1D, magenta curve). Fig. 1E exhibits the crystallinity and phase purity of PPCDs before and after interactions with Ag+ ions. Here, both curves at 23° are evident as a broad band, which is a sign of the amorphous nature of carbon dots. The lattice planes of silver ions (111), (200), and (222) were found to have three additional strong and powerful peaks at 38.9°, 44.7°, and 64.8°, respectively.

For the sensitivity analysis of PPCDs at pH values of 5.1, 7.2, and 10, Fig. 2 displays the selectivity and linearity responses. The increasing quantities of silver ions have caused an increase in the peak intensity of the PPCD(Fig. 2A-C)at pH-5.1, pH-7.2, and pH-10. Silver ions were detected with a LOD of 7.8, 0.758, and 0.340 μM in 5.1, 7.2, and 10 pH conditions, respectively. Despite the fact that the sensitivity analysis reports were usually similar, significant differences were found in the linear responses of the silver ions (Fig. 2D). The inset images show a considerable colour development of PPCDs with various concentrations of the silver ions, however, at 7.2 and 10 pH, significant colour was discerned in the PPCDs vials due to the strong coordination of phenoxide and carboxylate anions with Ag+ ions, while in the acidic medium, a considerable hue was not observed in the vials due to the inaccessibility of oxygen element's lone pairs, which may cause poor coordination between OH and COOH functional groups and silver ions. As shown in Fig. 2E & F, several potential interferences were evaluated in this regard using the PPCDs and silver ions. Among the various coexisting metal ions, the PPCDs absorbance was increased with the silver ion treatment only. A large brown colour enhancement with the addition of silver ions is also seen in the inset figure of Fig. 2F, indicating the existence of selectivity towards exclusively silver ions. A POC kit was developed using PPCDs on a silica gel strip (Fig. 3 A), and a significant, intense band appeared on the strips after addition of the silver ions with PPCDs at pH 5.1 (ii), 7.2 (iii), and 10 (iv), whereas no band was observed on the blank strip (i). Three pHconditions produced excellent bands, although the more pronounced dark colour bands were produced under neutral pH. The PPCDs and silver ions binding affinities at pH 7.2 are shown in Fig. 3B. At pH 10, 7.2, and 5.1, the presence of Ag+ ions accelerated the reaction rate 110, 87, and 42 times, respectively (Fig. S3). After the silver ions were added, the band gap properties also reduced, going from 3.52 to 3.15 eV. (Fig. 3C). FTIR results were also examined at specific wavelength regions, and significant peak shiftings were observed from 3560 to 3420 cm−1, 1735 to 1765 cm−1, in OH (Fig. 3D) and COOH (Fig. 3E) stretching frequencies, respectively, after interaction with silver ions. Noteworthy, two new bands appeared at 1400, and 825 cm−1 for OH bending (Fig. 3F), and COAg metal vibrations, respectively (Fig. 3G). According to the results discussed above, silver ions were coordinated with the COOH and OH functions, and band colour enhancement was produced by the LMCT complex by an electron transfer process (Fig. 3G). Finally, HO–/COO– to Ag+ ionic interactions in neutral and basic fluids improved the band appearance.

In conclusion, PPCDs that were intended to detect Ag+ were effectively synthesized and characterized. Excellent selectivity and sensitivity were found at specific pH levels. The ligand (PPCDs) and metal ions (Ag+) may form the LMCT complex, which may be the reason of this. A distinct black colour band was visible at three different pH values, and the developed POC kits produced identical results. The development of a sensor system and POC kits for the detection of silver ions using PPCDs has been successful.

Balathoti Sunitha Devi: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Bugude Laxmi: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ch. Linga Raju: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gorrepati Rosaiah: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Ankireddy Seshadri Reddy: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: No funding was received to perform this research work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.133872.
